questions.explanation: Transaction logs record changes (redo/undo information) so the DBMS can recover committed work and roll back incomplete work after failures.

options.A: To store query execution plans for faster reuse
options.B: To record changes so the database can recover to a consistent state after a crash
options.C: To keep a copy of all indexes for quick rebuilds
options.D: To compress table data to save disk space

questions.question: In a database management system (DBMS), what is the primary purpose of the transaction log (e.g., WAL in PostgreSQL, transaction log in SQL Server)?

questions.explanation: 'top' provides a live view of CPU, memory, and process activity, useful for diagnosing DB server resource pressure.
questions.question: On Linux/Unix, which command is commonly used to view real-time CPU and memory usage to quickly assess database host load?

questions.explanation: A foreign key ensures referenced values exist in the parent table (or are NULL if allowed), enforcing referential integrity.

options.A: Uniqueness of values in a column
options.B: That a column cannot contain NULL values
options.C: Referential integrity between a child table and a parent table

questions.question: In data modeling, what does a FOREIGN KEY constraint enforce?

questions.explanation: InnoDB supports transactions, row-level locking, and crash recovery, making it the standard ACID-capable engine.
questions.question: In MySQL, which storage engine is commonly used by default and supports ACID transactions?

questions.explanation: work_mem is allocated per sort/hash operation (per node), and insufficient values can cause disk spills during sorts/joins.
questions.question: In PostgreSQL, which configuration parameter primarily controls how much memory is used for each sort/hash operation before spilling to disk?

questions.explanation: Query Store persists query texts, plans, and runtime statistics over time, enabling performance regression analysis.
questions.question: In SQL Server, which feature provides a built-in way to capture and query historical performance metrics (CPU, waits, I/O) for troubleshooting over time?

questions.explanation: With (customer_id, order_date), the index can efficiently seek by customer_id and then read rows already ordered by order_date.

options.A: A composite B-tree index on (customer_id, order_date)
options.B: A composite B-tree index on (order_date, customer_id)

questions.question: A query frequently filters on (customer_id) and then sorts by (order_date). Which index best supports both the filter and the sort in most relational databases?

questions.explanation: PITR requires restoring a base backup and then replaying archived WAL to a target time/LSN before the unwanted change, then promoting.

options.A: Restore the base backup and replay WAL until just before 14:05, then promote the instance
options.B: Restore only the latest WAL files without the base backup
options.C: Run VACUUM FULL to undo the DELETE
options.D: Rebuild indexes and restart PostgreSQL to revert the data

questions.question: You need point-in-time recovery (PITR) for a PostgreSQL database after an accidental DELETE at 14:05. You have a base backup from last night and continuous archiving enabled. What is the correct recovery approach?

questions.explanation: Asynchronous replication can be lagged; ensuring read-after-write requires primary reads or waiting until a replica has applied the transaction (e.g., GTID/position-based checks).

options.A: Send all reads to replicas and increase replica count
options.B: Use read-after-write consistency by routing that user's reads to the primary (or to a replica only after confirming it has applied the relevant GTID/position)
options.C: Disable binary logging to reduce replication overhead
options.D: Switch replicas to statement-based replication to make them faster

questions.question: A MySQL primary is overloaded by read traffic. You add replicas for read scaling, but the application must never read stale data immediately after a user updates their profile. Which strategy best addresses this requirement?

questions.explanation: Synchronous replication minimizes data loss (near-zero RPO) and automatic failover reduces downtime (low RTO) compared to async or backup-only approaches.

options.B: Asynchronous replication to a standby with manual failover
options.C: Synchronous replication with automatic failover (e.g., SQL Server Always On synchronous commit / PostgreSQL synchronous standby with a failover manager)
options.D: Single server with RAID-10 and a UPS

questions.question: You are designing HA/DR for a mission-critical database with an RPO near zero and an RTO under 1 minute across two data centers. Which architecture most directly meets these targets?
